volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.1.265)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Vt vos in vostris voltis mercimoniis
sections.translation: As , in purchasing and selling your merchandize [*] , you are desirous to render me propitious to your bargains , and that I should assist you in all things ; and as both in foreign countries and at home , you desire me to turn to the best advantage the business and the accounts of you all ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.6
sections.original: bonoque atque amplo auctare perpetuo lucro
sections.translation: and that with fair and ample profit , without end , I should crown the venture both which you have begun , and which you shall begin ; and as you wish me to delight you and all yours with joyous news [*] — these tidings will I bring , that I may announce them to you , things which in especial are for your common interest

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.11
sections.original: ( nam vos quidem id iam scitis concessum et datum
sections.translation: ( for already do you know , indeed , that it has been given and assigned to me by the other Divinities , to preside over news and profit ): as you would wish me to favour and promote these things , that lasting gain may ever be forthcoming for you ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: ita huic facietis fabulae silentium
sections.translation: so shall you give silence for this play , and so shall you be fair and upright judges here , all of you . Now , by whose command , and for what reason I am come , I ’ ll tell you , and at the sane time , myself , I will disclose my name . By the command of Jupiter I am come ; my name is Mercury [*] .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: pater huc me misit ad vos oratum meus ,
sections.translation: My father has sent me hither to you to entreat , although , what should as his commands be enjoined on you , he knew that you would do , inasmuch as he knew full well that you venerate and fear himself , as is befitting Jupiter .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.24
sections.original: verum profecto hoc petere me precario
sections.translation: But , certainly , he bade me ask this of you with entreaty , in gentle tones , and in bland accents . For , in fact , this Jupiter , by whose command I am come , dreads a mishap [*] not less than any one of you . Born of a mortal mother , a mortal sire , it is not reasonable to be surprised if he has apprehensions for himself .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: atque ego quoque etiam , qui Iovis sum filius ,
sections.translation: And I too , as well , who am the son of Jupiter , through my relationship to my father , stand in dread of ill . Therefore , in peace am I come to you , and peace do I bring . I wish a thing to be asked of you that ’ s reasonable and feasible ; for , reasonable things to ask of the reasonable , a reasonable mediator have I been sent .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: nam iniusta ab iustis impetrari non decet ,
sections.translation: For from the reasonable it is not right to ask things unreasonable ; whereas from the unreasonable to ask things reasonable , is sheer folly , since these unrighteous persons are ignorant of what is right , and observe it not . Now then , all lend your attention here to the things which I shall say . What we wish , you ought to wish as well :

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: et ego et pater de vobis et re publica ;
sections.translation: both I and my father have well deserved of you and of your state . But why should I mention how in Tragedies I have seen others , such as Neptune , Valour , Victory , Mars , Bellona , making mention of the good services which they had done you ? Of all these benefits , the ruler of the Deities , my sire , was the founder .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.46
sections.original: sed mos numquam illi fuit patri meo ,†
sections.translation: But this has never been the habit of my father , to throw in your teeth what good he has done unto the good . He thinks that this is gratefully returned by you to him , and that he bestows these blessings on you deservedly , which he does bestow .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: Nunc quam rem oratum huc veni primum proloquar ,
sections.translation: Now , the matter which I came here to ask , I ’ ll first premise , after that I ’ ll tell the subject of this Tragedy . Why have you contracted your brows ? Is it because I said that this would be a Tragedy ? I am a God , and I ’ ll change it . This same , if you wish it , from a Tragedy I ’ ll make to be

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: comoedia ut sit omnibus isdem vorsibus .
sections.translation: a Comedy , with all the lines the same . Whether would ye it were so , or not ? But I ’ m too foolish ; as though I didn ’ t know , who am a God , that you so wish it ; upon this subject I understand what your feelings are . I ’ ll make this to be a mixture — a Tragi-comedy [*] .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: nam me perpetuo facere ut sit comoedia ,
sections.translation: For me to make it entirely to be a Comedy , where Kings and Gods appear , I do not deem right . What then ? Since here the servant has a part as well , just as I said , I ’ ll make it to be a Tragi-comedy . Now Jupiter has ordered me to beg this of you ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.65
sections.original: ut conquaestores singula in subsellia
sections.translation: that the inspectors [*] should go among each of the seats throughout the whole theatre [*] , amid the spectators , that , if they should see any suborned applauders of any actor , there should in the theatre be taken away from them the pledge of their coats , as a security for their good behaviour .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.69
sections.original: sive quí ambissint palmam histrionibus
sections.translation: But if any should solicit the palm of victory for the actors , or if for any artist , whether by written letters , or whether any person himself should solicit personally , or whether by messenger ; or if the Aediles , too , should unfairly adjudge to any one the reward ; Jupiter has commanded the law to be the same as if he had sought by solicitation an appointment for himself or for another .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.75
sections.original: virtute dixit vos victores vivere ,
sections.translation: By valour has he declared that you exist as victors , not by canvassing or unfair dealing . Why any the less should there be the same principle for the player , which there is for the greatest man ? By merit , not by favourers , ought we to seek our ends . He who does aright has ever favourers enough , if there is honesty in them in whose disposal this matter [*] rests .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.81
sections.original: hoc quoque etiam mihi pater in mandatis dedit ,
sections.translation: This , too , he directed me likewise in his injunctions , that there should be inspectors over the players ; that , he who should have procured suborned persons to applaud himself , or he who should have contrived for another give less satisfaction ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.85
sections.original: eius órnamenta et corium uti conciderent .
sections.translation: from the same they might strip off his dress and leather [*] mask . I don ’ t wish you to be surprised , for what reason Jupiter now concerns himself about actors . Don ’ t be surprised , Jupiter himself is about to take part in this play . Why are you wondering at this ? As though , indeed , a new thing were now mentioned , that Jupiter takes to the calling of a player .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.91
sections.original: etiam , histriones anno cum in proscaenio hic
sections.translation: But a year since [*] , when here on the stage the actors invoked Jupiter , he came ; he aided them . Besides , surely in Tragedy he has a place . This play , I say , Jupiter himself will take a part in this day ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.95
sections.original: et ego una cum illo . nunc vos animum advortite ,
sections.translation: and I together with him . Now do you give attention while I shall relate to you the subject of this Comedy .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.97
sections.original: Haec urbs est Thebae . in illisce habitat aedibus
sections.translation: This city is Thebes ; in that house there ( pointing ) , Amphitryon [*] dwells , born at Argos , of an Argive sire ; whose wife is Alcmena , daughter of Electryon .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.100
sections.original: is nunc Amphitruo praefectust legionibus ,
sections.translation: This Amphitrvon is now the general of the Theban troops ; for between the Teleboans and the Theban people there is war . He , before he departed hence for the expedition , left his wife Alcmena pregnant . But I believe that you already know how my father is disposed how free in these affairs he has been ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.105
sections.original: quam liber harum rerum multarum siet
sections.translation: and how great a lover of many a woman , if any object once has captivated him . Unknown to her husband , he began to love Alcmena , and took temporary possession of her person for himself , and made her pregnant , too , by his embrace .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.110
sections.original: nunc de Alcumena ut rem teneatis rectius ,
sections.translation: Now , that more fully you may understand the matter with respect to Alcmena , she is pregnant by both ; both by her husband and by supreme Jupiter And my father is now lying here ( he points to the house ) in-doors with her ; and for this reason is this night made longer , while he is taking this pleasure with her whom he desires .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.115
sections.original: sed ita adsimulavit se , quasi Amphitruo siet .
sections.translation: But he has so disguised himself , as though he were Amphitryon . Now , that you may not be surprised at this dress of mine , inasmuch as I have come out here this way in servile garb , an ancient and an antique circumstance , made new , will I relate to you , by reason of which I have come to you attired in this new fashion ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.120
sections.original: nam méus pater intus núnc est eccum Iuppiter ;
sections.translation: for lo ! my father Jupiter , now in the house , changes himself into the likeness of Amphitryon , and all the servants who see him think it is he , so shifting in his shape does he render himself when he chooses . I have taken on myself the form of the servant Sosia ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.125
sections.original: qui cum Amphitruone abiit hinc in exercitum ,
sections.translation: who has gone hence together with Amphitryon on the expedition , that I may be able to serve my father in his amour , and that the servants may not be enquiring who I am , when they see me here frequenting oft the house . Now , as they will suppose me a servant and their fellow-servant ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.130
sections.original: haud quisquam quaeret qui siem aut quid venerim .
sections.translation: not any one will enquire who I am , or why I ’ m come . My father , now in-doors , is gratifying his inclination , and is embracing her of whom he is especially enamoured . What has been done there at the army , my father is now relating to Alcmena . She , who really is with a paramour , thinks that he is her own husband . There , my father is now

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.136
sections.original: memorat , legiones hostium ut fugaverit ,
sections.translation: relating how he has routed the legions of the enemy ; how he has been enriched with abundant gifts . Those gifts which there were given to Amphitryon , we have carried off ; what he pleases , my father easily performs .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.140
sections.original: nunc hodie Amphitruo veniet huc ab exercitu
sections.translation: Now will Amphitryon come hither this day from the army , his servant too , whose form I am bearing . Now , that you may be able the more easily to distinguish between us , I always shall carry these little wings here ( pointing ) upon my broad-brimmed cap ; then besides , for my father there will be a golden tuft

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.145
sections.original: sub petaso : id signum Amphitruoni non erit .
sections.translation: beneath his cap ; that mark will not be upon Amphitryon . These marks no one of these domestics will be able to see ; but you will see them . But yonder is Sosia , the servant of Amphitryon ; he is now coming yonder from the harbour , with a lantern .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.150
sections.original: abigam iam ego illum advenientem ab aedibus .
sections.translation: I will now drive him , as he arrives , away from the house . Attend , it will be worth the while of you spectators , for Jupiter and Mercury to perform here the actors ’ part .

sections.label: Section 1.1.153
sections.original: Qui me álter est audácior homo aút qui confidéntior ,
sections.translation: What other person is there more bold than I , or who more stout of heart , who know the humours of young men [*] , and who am walking at this hour of night alone ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.155
sections.original: quid faciam nunc , si tres viri me in carcerem compegerint ?
sections.translation: What shall I do , if now the officers of the watch [*] should thrust me into prison . To-morrow shall I be dealt out from there [*] , just as though from a store-closet , for a whipping ; nor will it be allowed me to plead my cause , nor will there be a bit of aid from my master ; nor will there be a person but that they will imagine , all of them , that I am deserving .

sections.label: Section 1.1.159_160
sections.original: íta quasi incudém me miserum hómines octo válidi caedant :
sections.translation: And so will eight sturdy fellows be thumping on wretched me just like an anvil ; in this way , just come from foreign parts , I shall be received with hospitality by the public . The inconsiderateness of my master compels me to this , who has packed me off from the harbour at this time of night whether I would or no .

sections.label: Section 1.1.165
sections.original: nonne ídem hoc luci me míttere potuit ?
sections.translation: Couldn ’ t he as well have sent me here by daylight ? For this reason , is servitude to a man of high station a greater hardship ; for this reason is the servant of a wealthy man the more wretched : both night and day , without ceasing , there is enough , and more than enough of work for him ; for doing or for saying occasion is ever arising , so that you can ’ t be at rest .

sections.label: Section 1.1.170
sections.original: ípse dominus díves , operis ét laboris expers ,
sections.translation: The master , abounding in servants [*] , and free from labour himself ; thinks that whatever he happens to choose , can be done ; he thinks that just , and reckons not what the labour is ; nor will he ever consider whether he commands a thing that ’ s reasonable or unreasonable . Wherefore , in servitude many hardships do befall us ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.175
sections.original: habéndum et ferúndum hoc onúst cum labóre .
sections.translation: in pain this burden must be borne and endured .

sections.label: Section 1.1.176
sections.original: Satiúst me queri íllo modó servitútem :
sections.translation: ’ Twere with better reason for me to complain of servitude after this fashion ; I , who to-day was free , and whom my father is now employing as a slave : this fellow is complaining , who was born a slave .

sections.label: Section 1.1.180
sections.original: Sum véro verna vérbero : num número mi in mentém fuit ,
sections.translation: Really I am a rascal beyond a doubt ; for only this moment it has suggested itself to me , that on my arrival I should give thanks , and address the Gods for their kindnesses vouchsafed . For surely , by my troth , if they were only desirous to give me a return according to my deserts , they would commission some person on my arrival soundly to box my ears , since those kindnesses which they have done me I have held as worthless and of no value .

sections.label: Section 1.1.185
sections.original: Facit ille quod volgo haud solent , ut quid se sit dignum sciat .
sections.translation: He does what people are not generally in the habit of doing , in knowing what his deserts are .

sections.label: Section 1.1.186
sections.original: Quod numquam opinatus fui neque alius quisquam civium
sections.translation: What I never expected , nor any one else of my townsmen , to befall him , that same has come to pass , for us to come home safe and sound . Victorious , the enemy conquered , the troops are returning home , this very mighty war brought to an end , and the enemy slain .

sections.label: Section 1.1.190
sections.original: quod multa Thebano poplo acerba obiecit funera ,
sections.translation: A city that has caused many a bitter death for the Theban people , that same has been conquered by the strength and valour of our soldiers , and taken by storm , under the command and conduct of my master Amphitryon in especial . With booty , territory , and glory [*] , too , has he loaded his fellow-citizens , and for Creon , King of Thebes , has he firmly fixed his sway .

sections.label: Section 1.1.195
sections.original: me a portu praemisit domum , ut haec nuntiem uxori suae ,
sections.translation: From the harbour he has sent me before him to his house that I may bear these tidings to his wife , how he has promoted the public good by his guidance , conduct , and command . This now will I consider , in what manner I shall address her , when I ’ ve arrived there . If I tell a falsehood . I shall be doing as I am accustomed after my usual wont ; for when they were fighting with all their might , then with all my might I ran away .

sections.label: Section 1.1.200
sections.original: verum quasi adfuerim tamen simulabo atque audita eloquar .
sections.translation: But still I shall pretend as though I was present , and I ’ ll tell her what I heard . But in what manner and with what expressions it is right for me to tell my story , I still wish first to consider here with myself . ( He assumes an attitude of thought .) In these terms will I give this narrative . In the first place , when we arrived there , when first we made land , Amphitryon immediately made choice of the powerful men among the chieftains .

sections.label: Section 1.1.205
sections.original: eos legat , Telobois iubet sententiam ut dicant suam :
sections.translation: Those he despatched on the embassy , and bade them tell his mind to the Teleboans ; that if without constraint and without warfare they should be ready to deliver up what was plundered and the plunderers , and if they should be ready to restore what they had carried off , he would immediately conduct the army homewards , that the Greeks would depart from their territory , and that he would grant peace and quietness to them : but if they should be otherwise disposed , and not concede the things which he demanded ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.210
sections.original: sese igitur summa vi virisque eorum oppidum oppugnassere .
sections.translation: he , in consequence , would attack their city with extreme violence and with his men . When the embassadors had repeated these things , which Amphitryon had enjoined , in order to the Teleboans , being men stout of heart , relying on their valour , and confident in their prowess , they rebuked our embassadors very rudely . They answered that they were able in warfare to protect themselves and theirs , and that at once they must lead the army with all haste out of their territories .

sections.label: Section 1.1.216
sections.original: haec ubi legati pertulere , Amphitruo castris ilico
sections.translation: When the embassadors brought back this message , straightway Amphitryon drew out all his army from the encampment ; on the other side , the Teleboans led forth their legions from the town , furnished with most gorgeous arms . After they had gone forth on either side in full array ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.220
sections.original: dispertiti viri , dispertiti ordines ,
sections.translation: the soldiers were marshalled , the ranks were formed . We , after our manner and usage , drew up our legions ; the enemy , too , drew up their legions facing us . Then either general went forth into the mid-space beyond the throng of the ranks , and they parleyed together .

sections.label: Section 1.1.225
sections.original: convenit , victi utri sint eo proelio ,
sections.translation: It was agreed between them , that , which ever side should be conquered in that battle , they should surrender up their city , lands , altars , hearths . and theraselves . After that was done , the trumpets on either side gave the signal ; the earth re-echoed , they raised a shout on either side . Each general , both upon this side and on that , offered vows to Jupiter , and then encouraged his troops .

sections.label: Section 1.1.231
sections.original: tum pro se quisque id quod quisque potest et valet
sections.translation: Each man according to his ability does that which each one can and has the strength to do ; he smites with his falchion ; the weapons crash ; the welkin bellows with the uproar of the men ; of breaths and pantings a cloud is formed ; men fall by wounds inflicted by men .

sections.label: Section 1.1.235
sections.original: denique , ut voluimus , nostra superat manus :
sections.translation: At length , as we desired , our troops conquered ; the foe fell in numbers ; ours , on the other hand , pressed on ; firm in our strength , we were victorious . But still not one betook himself to flight , nor yet gave way at his post , but standing there [*] he waged the combat .

sections.label: Section 1.1.240
sections.original: animam omittunt prius quam loco demigrent :
sections.translation: Sooner than quit the spot , they parted with their lives ; each , as he stood , lay there and kept his rank in death . When my master Amphitryon saw this , at once he ordered the cavalry on the right to charge . The cavalry obeyed directly ; from the right wing ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.245
sections.original: cúm clamore involant ímpetu alacri ,
sections.translation: with a tremendous shout , with brisk onset they rushed on ; and rightfully did they slaughter and trample down the impious forces of the foe .

sections.label: Section 1.1.248
sections.original: Numquam étiam quicquam adhúc verborum est prólocutus pérperam :
sections.translation: Not even one word of these has he yet uttered correctly ; for I was there in the battle personally , and my father too , when it was fought .

sections.label: Section 1.1.250
sections.original: Perduelles penetrant se in fugam ; ibi nostris animus additust :
sections.translation: The enemy betook themselves to flight . Then was new spirit added to our men , the Teleboans flying , with darts were their bodies filled , and Amphitryon himself , with his own hand , struck off the head of Pterelas their king . This battle was being fought there even from the morning till the evening . This do I the better remember for this reason ; because on that day I went without my breakfast .

sections.label: Section 1.1.255
sections.original: sed proelium id tandem diremit nox interventu suo .
sections.translation: But night at last , by its interposing , cut short this combat . The next day , the chiefs came weeping from the city to us at the camp . With covered hands [*] , they entreated us to pardon their offences ; and they all surrendered rendered up themselves . and all things divine and human , their city and their children , into the possession and unto the disposal of the Theban people .

sections.label: Section 1.1.260
sections.original: post ob virtutem ero Amphitruoni patera donata aurea est ,
sections.translation: Lastly , by reason of his valour , a golden goblet was presented to my master Amphitryon , from which king Pterelas [*] had been used to drink . These things I ’ ll thus tell my mistress . I ’ ll now proceed to obey my master ’ s order and to betake me home .

sections.label: Section 1.1.263
sections.original: Áttat , illic húc iturust . íbo ego illi óbviam ,
sections.translation: Heyday ! he ’ s about to come this way ; I ’ ll go meet him ; and I ’ ll not permit this fellow at any time to-day to approach this house .

sections.label: Section 1.1.265
sections.original: quando imago est huius in me , certum est hominem eludere .
sections.translation: Since I have his form upon myself , I ’ m resolved to play the fellow off . And indeed , since I have taken upon me his figure and his station , it is right for me likewise to have actions and manners like to his . Therefore it befits me to be artful , crafty , very cunning , and by his own weapon , artfulness , to drive him from the door .
